name: theme-image
title: Theme Image
description: An image that swaps with the active Commons theme — a seal or logo drawn for a light page often disappears on a dark one, and a white mark vanishes in high contrast (which is light-based). Give it up to three sources (light, dark, highContrast) and the one matching the theme is shown: an explicit data-theme on the root wins, with a prefers-color-scheme / prefers-contrast fallback for the system setting. Omit a variant to reuse another, so a single light source works everywhere. The swap itself is the .cui-theme-image rules in @21stgov/commons-core (already in every Commons install), mirroring the token themes' own selectors — so the same markup works on the framework-agnostic path.
useWhen: A municipal seal, logo, or wordmark that needs a different rendering per theme (color on light, white on dark, black in high contrast)
Any decorative or informational image whose drawn colors stop working when the theme changes
Header identity marks next to a HeaderTitle, or partner logos in a footer
avoidWhen: Photographs and images that read fine on every background (use a plain )
Icons that can inherit text color (use an inline SVG with currentColor instead — one asset, zero swapping)
Content images that differ per theme in meaning, not just rendering (serve the right image, don't theme-swap it)

keyboard: Hidden variants are display: none, so they are out of the accessibility tree and the tab order; the alt text is announced exactly once
Decorative by default (alt=""); pass a real alt when the image carries meaning that isn't in adjacent text
notes: All variants share one alt and one set of attributes, so whichever variant the theme shows announces identically.
The high-contrast theme is light-based: point highContrast at the color or black mark, not the white one, or let it fall back to light.

The CSS lives in @21stgov/commons-core (theme-image.css), keyed to the same selectors as the token themes — including the prefers-color-scheme and prefers-contrast fallbacks for visitors on the system setting.
In forced-colors mode the images render as-is (they are content, not CSS paint), and the swap keys off the page's own theme.
